mnemonics.title: Production System Components
mnemonics.content: WPA - Working memory, Production memory, Action! (Inference Engine controls)

acronyms.usage: Stores current facts and assertions about the problem state
Stores the collection of IF-THEN production rules
Controls the recognize-act cycle of matching and execution
Efficient pattern matching algorithm that networks rule conditions
Pattern used to represent facts in working memory

visualTips.visualization: Draw an IF-THEN as a flowchart: Condition box → Decision diamond → Action box. Think of it as 'IF this condition EXISTS, THEN DO this action'.
Draw a circular cycle: MATCH (circle) → CONFLICT RESOLUTION (diamond) → ACT (rectangle) → back to MATCH. Each arrow shows the flow of control.
Forward: Facts → Rules → New Facts (left to right arrow). Backward: Goal ← Rules ← Facts (right to left arrow). Remember: Forward starts with DATA, Backward starts with GOAL.
Think of a decision tree where each node is a test. Facts flow through the network, activating rules when all conditions are satisfied - like water flowing through branching pipes.

analogies.analogy: Think of a production system like a factory assembly line. Working Memory is the conveyor belt with current items (facts), Production Memory is the set of instructions (rules) posted at workstations, and Inference Engine is the foreman directing which instruction to apply next.

Like a detective gathering clues - starting with known evidence and applying rules of deduction to reach conclusions. Each new fact potentially leads to more facts, like following a trail of breadcrumbs.
Like a doctor diagnosing a patient - starting with a suspected condition (goal) and ordering tests (working backward through rules) to confirm or rule out the diagnosis.
Like a judge in a courtroom with multiple lawyers shouting arguments. The judge (conflict resolver) must decide which argument (rule) to hear first based on established criteria.

Like a caching system in a library. Instead of searching every book for every query, the library keeps indexes (cached partial matches) to quickly find relevant books - much faster than searching from scratch each time.
